hero.title: Welcome — I’m Kaan.
hero.subtitle: I design digital learning experiences and develop the software behind them.

about.title: About Me
paragraphs: Hi! I’m Kaan, a junior at Boğaziçi University studying Computer Education and Educational Technology (CET). I graduated from Mehmet Tekinalp High School in 2019 and currently live in Istanbul. I am passionate about technology and education, and I am always curious to learn more.

I have been interested in technology and computers since childhood. I have developed skills in HTML, JavaScript, Python, C/C++, and more. I am also experienced in using office applications, various operating systems, and tools like Articulate for creating interactive projects.
I aim to apply my skills in technology and education to create meaningful learning experiences. I am always looking for opportunities to grow, collaborate, and contribute to innovative projects.

footAngle.title: FootAngle - Learning Angles Through Football
footAngle.shortDescription: FootAngle is an interactive learning module where learners take the role of a football player to understand and apply angle concepts through realistic training scenarios.
paragraphs: FootAngle is an interactive learning module where learners take the role of a football player to understand and apply angle concepts through realistic training scenarios.

Developed using Articulate Storyline, this module combines instructional design principles with scenario-based learning. Learners actively adjust shooting angles, analyze ball trajectories, and receive immediate feedback based on their decisions.

The project integrates interactive triggers, variables, conditional branching, and visual feedback elements to create an engaging and immersive learning experience. It demonstrates the application of gamification strategies and experiential learning within a digital learning environment.

horseRacing.title: Horse Racing Game
horseRacing.shortDescription: Horse Racing Game is a Scratch-based game where players predict race outcomes and experience basic game mechanics through interactive play.
horseRacing.description: A horse racing prediction game developed using Scratch. The player selects a horse and earns points based on randomly generated race outcomes. The project demonstrates fundamental game mechanics, including variables, conditionals, and event-driven programming.

spireSlayer.title: Spire Slayer
spireSlayer.shortDescription: A command-line interpreter and state management system in C for a roguelike deckbuilder game. This project highlights strict grammar validation, dynamic memory management, and robust data structures.
paragraphs: This project is a robust command-line interpreter developed in C that simulates the complex state management of a roguelike deckbuilding game. Operating through a continuous read-parse-respond loop, the program processes user commands to manage a player's progression, including tracking health, accumulating gold, and executing combat mechanics against enemies.

Going beyond basic state tracking, the interpreter enforces strict input validation and grammar rules, ensuring malformed commands are gracefully rejected without crashing. The game maintains a central, persistent state architecture to track both static boundaries, like a limited potion belt, and dynamically scaling inventories, such as the player's evolving deck and relic collections.

Technically, the project demonstrates proficiency in low-level memory management using pointers, malloc, and realloc to prevent memory leaks during continuous array resizing. The integration of a complex "Codex" system for enemy tracking further highlights the application's capability to handle nested data structures and complex game logic seamlessly.

schedSim.title: SchedSim
schedSim.shortDescription: A CPU process scheduling simulator written entirely in GNU Assembly. This project showcases bare-metal programming, manual memory management, and the implementation of five distinct scheduling algorithms without external C libraries.
paragraphs: SchedSim is a low-level CPU process scheduling simulator developed entirely in GNU Assembly (x86-64, AT&T syntax). The application accurately simulates the execution timeline of multiple processes by processing hardware-level clock cycles and visualizing the output string.

The simulator implements five distinct process scheduling algorithms to determine CPU allocation: First-Come First-Served (FCFS), Shortest Job First (SJF), Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF), Priority First (PF), and Round Robin (RR) with a custom circular queue. Each algorithm dynamically handles arrival times, burst times, and preemption rules to mimic realistic operating system behaviors.

Designed without reliance on the C standard library, the project interacts directly with the Linux kernel via system calls (syscall) for input and output operations. It features a custom ASCII-to-integer parser, manual register tracking, and contiguous memory struct layouts within the .bss section, demonstrating a deep understanding of computer architecture and system-level programming.

parkingVideo.title: Instructional Video: Parallel & L-Parking Tutorial
parkingVideo.shortDescription: A step-by-step parking guide combining POV and exterior shots to reduce cognitive load and enhance spatial awareness through multimedia learning principles.
parkingVideo.description: Designed as a multimedia learning tool, this project breaks down complex driving maneuvers (parallel and perpendicular parking) into digestible, step-by-step instructions. By strategically combining POV and exterior camera angles, the video aims to reduce cognitive load and enhance spatial awareness for learner drivers, delivering a clear and objective-driven educational experience.

2048.title: 2048 Game
2048.shortDescription: An enhanced 2048 puzzle game built with C++ and Qt. This project demonstrates event-driven architecture and state management by introducing a stack-based undo system and four custom game modes.
paragraphs: This project is a feature-rich variation of the popular 2048 sliding-tile puzzle game, developed using C++ and the Qt Framework. Played on a classic 4x4 grid, the objective is to strategically combine matching tiles to reach the elusive 2048 tile.

Going beyond the standard game, this version introduces four distinct game modes to challenge players. The Normal Mode offers the classic 2048 experience, while Hard Mode automatically makes a random valid move if the player remains inactive for 5 seconds. Additionally, Unlimited Mode allows players to continue playing after reaching 2048, and Unlimited Hard Mode combines endless gameplay with the pressure of the 5-second timer.

The game includes several key features designed for a seamless user experience. A built-in stack-based undo system allows players to revert to their previous state if they make a mistake. The intuitive graphical interface features keyboard controls, best score tracking, and interactive modal menus, all while demonstrating strong object-oriented programming principles, event-driven game logic, and efficient matrix manipulation.

frictionRally.title: Friction Rally: Interactive Science Module
frictionRally.shortDescription: Friction Rally is an Articulate Storyline e-learning module designed to teach middle school students the concept of friction force. It achieves this through a gamified racing experience where learners test how different road surfaces impact vehicle motion.
paragraphs: This scenario-based e-learning project was developed using Articulate Storyline to teach middle school science students about friction and motion. Blending pedagogical principles with automotive gamification, the module places the learner in the role of a rally race test engineer.

Users interactively explore how asphalt, dirt, and icy surfaces affect a vehicle's traction. The project utilizes custom motion paths, teeter, and spin animations to simulate real-world vehicle physics. Key technical features include variable-based personalization (custom user names) and complex trigger logic. A standout element is an interactive drag-and-drop simulation where students must apply their knowledge to select the correct tire (summer vs. winter tire) to ensure safety on an icy road scenario. The module concludes with a comprehensive assessment quiz featuring multiple-choice, hotspot, and matching questions, requiring a 60% passing score for successful completion.
